summary: Gruber shares his hands-on impressions of the iPhone 4 after Apple's keynote demo area. He focuses heavily on the 'retina display' technology, particularly the new production process that fuses the LCD and touchscreen glass, eliminating the air gap between them. This creates an effect where pixels appear painted on the surface rather than sitting beneath glass. He also notes the device's improved build quality, narrower form factor, wider camera lens, and the curious detail that all demo phones had Bing set as the default search engine.
key_points: The iPhone 4's new fused LCD-touchscreen eliminates the air gap, making pixels appear painted on the glass surface rather than beneath it
The device is both thinner and narrower than the 3GS, with the display spanning nearly the full width, making it feel smaller in hand
The stainless steel construction (used because it can function as an antenna, unlike aluminum) gives the phone incredible build density and rigidity
Apple demo phones in the media area all had Bing set as the search engine instead of Google — a notable detail Gruber flags without elaboration
money_quotes: Text renders like high quality print.
The effect is that the pixels appear to be painted on the surface of the phone; instead of looking at pixels under glass, it's like looking at pixels on glass.
Combined with the incredibly high pixel density, the overall effect is like "live print".
It's like a glossy magazine come to life.
The build quality is incredible. It feels dense and extremely rigid.
Google remains the default search engine in iOS 4, but on all the demo phones in the hands-on area for the media, the search engine was set to Bing.
key_insight: The iPhone 4's fused display technology, which eliminates the air gap between LCD and glass, creates a 'live print' effect that is as significant as the retina resolution itself.
